Web 3.0 won't be realised with many small projects operating within their own ecosystems. Interoperability at the data layer needs to be standardised like #PayID . Data must be user owned, maintained and hyper-referenced intrinsically to create a navigatable web of information.
Tools like #Blockchain, #ZKPs / #zkSNARKs, #SmartContracts, #Interledger and #IPFS are all pieces of the puzzle. Some may need to be iterated upon, some pieces may yet to be created.

A portal that can act as both the data transfer layer between Web 2.0 and Web 3.0 is the virtual (reality) world. A gamified, game-esque social environment can supersede the tech platforms by placing data at the user's hands, instead of behind closed doors.
This will require new digital economic models and mechanisms that solve for activities that result in win-lose dynamics and rent seeking, and instead are omni-win and anti-rivalrous.

As supply chains continue to be squeezed, fragmented and collapse, it becomes increasingly important to localise needs.

There are essentially three qualities and scales that must be considered:

1. Biological survival needs local - shelter, food, water, communication, medicine, safety - the degree to which these are met within the local context (bioregion) is the degree to which a populous survives.
2. Socio-political survival - the tensions of trade of resources, information and labour between bioregions
3. Cross cultural industrial thriving - the degree to which industrial technologies can be used to produce advanced quality of life products, is the degree to which a civilisation will thrive

These must all be radically scaled DOWN to smaller and smaller contexts meet the demands of an economy of scaling up. This is a complex relationship of dynamics between both bottom up (localism) and top down (degrowth) strategies.

And it looks like this.

@SketchpunkLabs Ethereal Engine makes it easy ;) we actually just this week introduced a new pattern allowing you to use reactive logic to ECS data changes inside systems - it's pretty snazzy.

@SketchpunkLabs There are two ways we currently use it.

1. We can define a component based on a query, which is added upon the query being met, removed otherwise.

https://t.co/dURRv9VVzd

This is our best example, listening both for state changes and component changes.

@SketchpunkLabs 2. We can define reactors for single components themselves, which are used to respond to any and all changes to the component data, for example: changing the size of an image, or it's src

https://t.co/RFJ8SFKQim

@SketchpunkLabs This allows us to use the react scheduler to splay updates across multiple frames, per entity, and define custom priority behaviour, to reduce frame lag upon large changes (such as updating a shader across the whole scene hierarchy in the first example).

@SketchpunkLabs Our whole engine is based on a data oriented design paradigm using bitECS, preserving access to the raw buffers for hotspots (netcode, transforms etc) yet we augmented this using hookstate as a data store,  allowing reactivity to data changes.
@SketchpunkLabs We have an alternative pattern which may be faster, a useQuery hook, but I'm still wrapping my head around it 😅

@SketchpunkLabs Fair enough! We're going to do a big push in the coming weeks for documentation and examples including a complete open source game built on the engine, utilising all these patterns. React did my head in until it didn't, now I'm addicted 😅

Diversity, Unity & Plurality, instead of Diversity, Equity & Inclusion

Defining the tension of diversity as a relationship (unity), rather than something that needs to be balanced (equity/inclusion), as well as explicating transcending differences (plurality).

Scarcity vs abundance. 

Equity (balancing equation, quantitative, implies imbalance) and inclusion (implying exclusion) as examples of scarcity.

Unity (noticing the whole and how parts relate) and plurality (noticing the parts as parts and of a whole) as examples of abundance.

Diversity is naturally abundant.

Diversity necessitates unity (shared goals, super-identity), not equity (individual goal, tension of direction), and thrives in plurality (feeling intrinsic worth and opportunities to take perspectives), not inclusion (pressure to feel accepted).

Ethereal Engine v1.0.0 has been released! 🥳🤯🎮

- Renamed "editor" to "studio"
- Migrate from socketio to primus

- Node 18 support

There's a difference between automation and augmentation.

The progression of AI can be an increasingly hands on process, or an increasingly hands off process.

Where do humans fit in to the process, is the exact question to ask that "alignment" as a concept fails to address.
Alignment implies attempting to parallelise two otherwise divergent paths, whereas what is actually needed is coevolving dynamic interplay between these two paths.
Finding a basin of attraction or schelling point around genuine willingness to engage in exploring this interplay, is "alignment".

There is no ends, there is only means. The product of "an aligned AI" is a complicated narrative to a complex scenario - instead process is what is needed.

Process => Project => Product

One of the most powerful and important parts of @ethereal_engine is the Project API. It allows modular and composable code to be installed and run very easily, you can make different tools that have a common runtime and spatial context, that can be made truly interoperable.
@ethereal_engine This can be seen as a whole new ecosystem, similar but distinct from npm, pip/anaconda, the Unity Asset Store, or even just github wrt the operating system.

@ethereal_engine Ethereal Engine IS a decentralized spatial operating system, which runs as a networked mesh of spatial content providers, all with their own individual realities, histories and stories.

@ethereal_engine By adopting standards like #USDZ, #GLTF, #WebXR, these become more loosly interoperable with the wider spatial web, while still maintaining graceful degredation to non immersive contexts, like 2D screens, audio interfaces, or anything that provides a portal to the digital world.

1 - identify a problem
2 - define the problem
3 - scope the problem
4 - find the extremes
5 - build tools to highlight and collapse the extremes into a workable set of dimensions
6 - trial many solutions in parallel
7 - implement the most fitted for specific cases
8 - repeat 4-7
